notes.title: Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Flower structure, gametophyte development, pollination, fertilisation and seed/fruit formation.

## 1. Flower & Gametophytes
- **Microsporogenesis:** pollen grains form in anther (microspore mother cell → meiosis → pollen). Mature pollen = 2-celled (vegetative + generative) or 3-celled.
- **Megasporogenesis:** in ovule, megaspore mother cell → meiosis → one functional megaspore → 7-celled, 8-nucleate embryo sac.

## 2. Pollination
- Self (autogamy, geitonogamy) vs cross (xenogamy). Agents: wind, water, insects.
- Outbreeding devices prevent self-pollination.

## 3. Fertilisation
- **Double fertilisation** (unique to angiosperms): one male gamete + egg → zygote (2n); other + 2 polar nuclei → PEN → endosperm (3n).

## 4. Post-fertilisation
- Ovule → seed; ovary → fruit. Endosperm nourishes embryo.
- **Apomixis** (seeds without fertilisation), **polyembryony** (more than one embryo).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Embryo sac is 7-celled but 8-nucleate.
- Endosperm is triploid (3n) due to triple fusion.
- Double fertilisation = syngamy + triple fusion.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Pollen: 2 or 3 celled; embryo sac 7-celled/8-nucleate.
- Double fertilisation → zygote (2n) + endosperm (3n).
- Ovule→seed, ovary→fruit.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember the embryo sac (7-celled, 8-nucleate) and ploidy of endosperm (3n) — repeated NEET questions.

notes.title: Human Reproduction — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Male & female reproductive systems, gametogenesis, menstrual cycle and pregnancy.

## 1. Male System
- Testes (seminiferous tubules — sperm; Leydig cells — testosterone; Sertoli cells — nourish sperm).
- **Spermatogenesis** produces 4 sperms per spermatogonium; controlled by FSH, LH, testosterone.

## 2. Female System
- Ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus. **Oogenesis** begins before birth; one ovum + polar bodies per cycle.

## 3. Menstrual Cycle (~28 days)
- Phases: menstrual, follicular (proliferative), **ovulation** (~day 14, LH surge), luteal (secretory).
- Hormones: FSH, LH, estrogen, progesterone. Corpus luteum secretes progesterone.

## 4. Fertilisation & Pregnancy
- Fertilisation in the ampulla of the fallopian tube. Implantation of blastocyst.
- Placenta: nourishment + hormones (hCG, hPL). Gestation ~9 months.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Ovulation is triggered by the LH surge (~day 14).
- Sertoli cells nourish sperm; Leydig cells make testosterone.
- Fertilisation occurs in the ampulla, not the uterus.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Spermatogenesis → 4 sperm; oogenesis → 1 ovum.
- LH surge → ovulation.
- Fertilisation in ampulla; placenta hormones.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the menstrual cycle hormone graph and the role of corpus luteum — very high yield.

notes.title: Reproductive Health — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Reproductive health, contraception, and assisted reproductive technologies.

## 1. Reproductive Health
- Total reproductive well-being. Programmes: RCH (reproductive & child health).

## 2. Contraception
- Natural (rhythm, withdrawal), barrier (condoms, diaphragm), IUDs (Cu-T), hormonal (pills), surgical (vasectomy/tubectomy).
- Ideal contraceptive: user-friendly, effective, reversible, no side effects.

## 3. MTP & STIs
- Medical Termination of Pregnancy (legalised, safe up to certain weeks).
- STIs: gonorrhoea, syphilis, AIDS, hepatitis-B — prevention through awareness.

## 4. Infertility & ART
- **ART:** IVF (test-tube baby, ZIFT, GIFT), ICSI, artificial insemination (AI).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- IVF: fertilisation outside body, embryo transferred (ZIFT into fallopian, IUT into uterus).
- Cu-T works by suppressing sperm motility/fertilising capacity.
- Lactational amenorrhoea is a natural method.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Contraceptive methods categories.
- ART: IVF, ZIFT, GIFT, ICSI.
- Common STIs.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Distinguish the ART techniques (ZIFT vs GIFT vs IUT) — a commonly confused NEET point.

notes.title: Principles of Inheritance and Variation — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Mendelian genetics, deviations, and chromosomal/genetic disorders.

## 1. Mendel's Laws
- Law of dominance, **law of segregation** (no exceptions), **law of independent assortment**.
- Monohybrid ratio 3:1 (phenotype), dihybrid 9:3:3:1.

## 2. Deviations from Mendelism
- **Incomplete dominance** (Mirabilis, 1:2:1), **co-dominance** (AB blood group), **multiple alleles** (ABO), **pleiotropy**, **polygenic inheritance** (skin colour).

## 3. Linkage & Chromosomal Theory
- Linked genes inherited together (Morgan). Recombination frequency maps genes.

## 4. Sex Determination & Disorders
- XX–XY (humans), XX–XO (insects), ZZ–ZW (birds).
- **Mendelian disorders:** haemophilia, colour blindness (X-linked recessive), sickle-cell anaemia, thalassaemia.
- **Chromosomal disorders:** Down (trisomy 21), Turner (XO), Klinefelter (XXY).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Law of segregation has no exceptions; independent assortment fails for linked genes.
- Colour blindness & haemophilia are X-linked recessive (more common in males).
- Down syndrome = trisomy of chromosome 21.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- 3:1 and 9:3:3:1 ratios.
- Incomplete vs co-dominance.
- Genetic & chromosomal disorders.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Practise pedigree analysis and the X-linked recessive inheritance pattern — frequent NEET questions.

notes.title: Molecular Basis of Inheritance — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
DNA as genetic material, replication, transcription, translation and gene regulation.

## 1. DNA Structure
- Watson–Crick double helix; antiparallel strands; base pairing A=T (2 H-bonds), G≡C (3 H-bonds).
- Chargaff's rule: A=T, G=C. One turn = 10 bp = 3.4 nm.

## 2. DNA as Genetic Material
- Griffith (transformation), Avery, Hershey–Chase (bacteriophage) — confirmed DNA.

## 3. Replication
- Semiconservative (Meselson–Stahl). Enzymes: helicase, DNA polymerase, ligase. Leading & lagging (Okazaki fragments) strands.

## 4. Central Dogma
- DNA → (transcription) → RNA → (translation) → protein.
- **Genetic code:** triplet, degenerate, non-overlapping, universal, with start (AUG) and stop codons.
- tRNA = adapter; ribosome = site of translation.

## 5. Gene Regulation & HGP
- **Lac operon** (inducible) in E. coli.
- Human Genome Project; DNA fingerprinting (VNTRs).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Replication is semiconservative.
- Genetic code is degenerate (one amino acid, many codons) but unambiguous.
- AUG = start codon (also methionine).

## 📝 Quick Recap
- A=T (2 bonds), G≡C (3 bonds).
- Semiconservative replication.
- Central dogma; lac operon.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Master the lac operon and properties of the genetic code — guaranteed NEET questions.

notes.title: Evolution — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Origin of life, evidence, mechanisms and Hardy–Weinberg principle.

## 1. Origin of Life
- Oparin–Haldane (chemical evolution); Miller–Urey experiment (organic molecules from inorganic).

## 2. Evidence for Evolution
- Homologous (divergent), analogous (convergent) organs; fossils; embryology; molecular evidence.

## 3. Theories
- Lamarck (inheritance of acquired characters — rejected), **Darwin** (natural selection, survival of the fittest), modern synthesis (mutation + recombination + natural selection).

## 4. Hardy–Weinberg Principle
- Allele frequencies remain constant (genetic equilibrium): p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
- Disturbed by: gene flow, genetic drift, mutation, selection, non-random mating.

## 5. Types of Selection
- Stabilising, directional, disruptive. Adaptive radiation (Darwin's finches).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Homologous = common ancestry (divergent); analogous = different ancestry (convergent).
- Hardy–Weinberg describes a NON-evolving population.
- Lamarckism (use & disuse) is rejected.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Oparin–Haldane; Miller–Urey.
- Darwin's natural selection.
- p² + 2pq + q² = 1.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the five factors that disturb Hardy–Weinberg equilibrium — direct NEET question.

notes.title: Human Health and Disease — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Diseases, the immune system, AIDS, cancer and drug abuse.

## 1. Pathogens & Diseases
- Bacterial (typhoid, pneumonia), viral (common cold), protozoan (malaria — Plasmodium, by female Anopheles), helminth (ascariasis, filariasis), fungal (ringworm).

## 2. Immunity
- **Innate** (non-specific barriers) vs **acquired** (specific, memory).
- Humoral (B-cells, antibodies) vs cell-mediated (T-cells).
- Antibody structure: H₂L₂ (two heavy, two light chains). Types: IgG, IgM, IgA, IgE, IgD.
- Active (own antibodies, vaccines) vs passive (ready-made, e.g. mother's milk).

## 3. AIDS & Cancer
- AIDS: HIV (retrovirus) attacks helper T-cells; spread by blood/sexual contact.
- Cancer: uncontrolled cell division (loss of contact inhibition); oncogenes; benign vs malignant (metastasis).

## 4. Drugs & Alcohol
- Opioids, cannabinoids, cocaine; addiction & dependence.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Malaria is caused by Plasmodium, transmitted by FEMALE Anopheles.
- Active immunity = own response (slow, long-lasting); passive = ready antibodies (fast, short).
- HIV targets helper T-lymphocytes.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Innate vs acquired immunity.
- Antibody = H₂L₂.
- AIDS (HIV), cancer (metastasis).

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn pathogen–disease–vector matching and active vs passive immunity — high frequency in NEET.

notes.title: Microbes in Human Welfare — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Useful microbes in food, industry, sewage treatment and agriculture.

## 1. In Household & Food
- Lactobacillus (curd), yeast (bread, alcohol), Propionibacterium (Swiss cheese), Aspergillus.

## 2. Industrial Products
- **Antibiotics:** Penicillin (Penicillium notatum — Fleming).
- **Organic acids, enzymes** (lipase, pectinase), **beverages** (fermentation by yeast).

## 3. Sewage Treatment
- Primary (physical) & secondary (biological — activated sludge, BOD reduction). Anaerobic digesters produce biogas.

## 4. Biofertilisers & Biocontrol
- **Biofertilisers:** Rhizobium (N fixation), Azospirillum, mycorrhiza (Glomus), cyanobacteria.
- **Biocontrol:** Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt), Trichoderma, Baculovirus.
- Biogas (mainly methane) by methanogens (e.g. Methanobacterium).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- High BOD = more polluted water (more organic matter).
- Penicillium gives penicillin; Streptomyces gives many antibiotics.
- Methanogens produce methane (biogas) and are found in rumen of cattle.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Lactobacillus (curd), yeast (alcohol/bread).
- Sewage: BOD, activated sludge.
- Biofertilisers & biocontrol agents.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Memorise microbe–product matching (organism → product) — almost always asked.

notes.title: Biotechnology: Principles & Processes — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Tools and processes of recombinant DNA technology.

## 1. Tools of rDNA Technology
- **Restriction enzymes** (molecular scissors; cut at palindromic sites, produce sticky ends).
- **DNA ligase** (joins fragments), **vectors** (plasmids, e.g. pBR322), **host** (E. coli).
- Selectable markers; PCR (amplification using Taq polymerase, primers).

## 2. Steps in rDNA
1. Isolate DNA. 2. Cut with restriction enzyme. 3. Insert into vector. 4. Transfer to host. 5. Culture & obtain product.

## 3. Bioreactors & Downstream
- Stirred-tank / sparged bioreactors for large-scale culture.
- Downstream processing: separation & purification of product.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Restriction enzymes recognise palindromic sequences and make staggered cuts (sticky ends).
- EcoRI is from E. coli; named by source organism.
- PCR uses heat-stable Taq polymerase.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Restriction enzyme + ligase + vector + host.
- pBR322 features.
- PCR steps: denaturation, annealing, extension.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Understand sticky ends and the role of each enzyme — conceptual NEET favourites.

notes.title: Biotechnology and its Applications — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Applications of biotechnology in agriculture, medicine and beyond.

## 1. Agriculture
- **Bt crops** (Bt cotton — cry genes, resist insects), **pest-resistant plants** (RNA interference against nematodes), golden rice (vitamin A).

## 2. Medicine
- **Genetically engineered insulin** (Humulin — by E. coli), gene therapy (ADA deficiency — first case), molecular diagnosis (PCR, ELISA).

## 3. Transgenic Animals & Ethics
- Transgenic animals for research, products. Biopiracy & patents; GEAC regulates GM research in India.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Bt toxin (cry protein) is inactive until it becomes active in the alkaline insect gut.
- Insulin made by rDNA is identical to human insulin (two chains A & B joined by disulfide bonds).
- RNAi silences specific mRNA.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Bt crops (cry genes); golden rice.
- rDNA insulin; gene therapy.
- RNAi; GEAC.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn Bt toxin mechanism and rDNA insulin production — commonly tested applications.

notes.title: Organisms and Populations — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Ecology basics: adaptations, population attributes and interactions.

## 1. Organism & Environment
- Abiotic factors (temperature, water, light, soil). Responses: regulate, conform, migrate, suspend.
- Adaptations (kangaroo rat, desert plants, etc.).

## 2. Population Attributes
- Birth rate, death rate, sex ratio, age pyramids, population density.
- **Growth models:** exponential (J-shaped, unlimited resources) dN/dt = rN; logistic (S-shaped, with carrying capacity K) dN/dt = rN(K−N)/K.

## 3. Population Interactions
- **Mutualism** (+/+), **competition** (−/−), **predation** (+/−), **parasitism** (+/−), **commensalism** (+/0), **amensalism** (−/0).
- Gause's competitive exclusion principle.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Logistic growth is more realistic (resources limited, S-shaped).
- Commensalism: one benefits, other unaffected (e.g. orchid on tree).
- Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population an environment supports.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- Exponential (J) vs logistic (S) growth.
- Interaction signs (+/−/0).
- Adaptations & population attributes.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Learn the sign combinations of population interactions with one example each — frequent NEET MCQ.

notes.title: Ecosystem — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Energy flow, food chains, ecological pyramids and nutrient cycling.

## 1. Structure & Function
- Components: producers, consumers, decomposers; abiotic.
- Productivity: GPP, NPP (= GPP − respiration), secondary productivity.

## 2. Energy Flow
- Unidirectional; **10% law** (only ~10% energy transferred to the next trophic level).
- Food chains (grazing & detritus) and food webs.

## 3. Ecological Pyramids
- Pyramid of number, biomass, energy. **Pyramid of energy is always upright.**
- Inverted biomass pyramid possible (e.g. sea — phytoplankton small biomass).

## 4. Nutrient Cycling & Succession
- Biogeochemical cycles (carbon, phosphorus — sedimentary).
- Ecological succession (primary on bare rock; climax community).

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- Energy pyramid is ALWAYS upright; biomass/number pyramids can be inverted.
- Only ~10% energy passes up each trophic level.
- Phosphorus cycle is sedimentary (no gaseous phase); carbon has a gaseous phase.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- NPP = GPP − respiration.
- 10% law; upright energy pyramid.
- Succession to climax.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Remember the energy pyramid is always upright and the 10% energy transfer rule.

notes.title: Biodiversity and Conservation — Complete NEET Notes
notes.description:
## Overview
Levels of biodiversity, its patterns, loss and conservation strategies.

## 1. Biodiversity Levels
- Genetic, species, ecological diversity.
- More diversity near the equator (latitudinal gradient); tropics richest.

## 2. Patterns & Importance
- Species–area relationship (S = CA^z). Rivet popper hypothesis.
- Ecosystem services: pollination, climate, oxygen.

## 3. Loss of Biodiversity
- Causes ('Evil Quartet'): habitat loss & fragmentation, over-exploitation, alien species invasion, co-extinctions.

## 4. Conservation
- **In-situ:** national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves, biodiversity hotspots, sacred groves.
- **Ex-situ:** zoos, botanical gardens, seed banks, cryopreservation.

## ⚠️ Common Traps
- In-situ = in natural habitat; ex-situ = outside natural habitat.
- Biodiversity is highest in tropical rainforests (Amazon).
- The 'Evil Quartet' are the four causes of biodiversity loss.

## 📝 Quick Recap
- 3 levels of biodiversity.
- Evil Quartet (loss causes).
- In-situ vs ex-situ conservation.

## 🎯 Exam Tip
Classify each conservation method as in-situ or ex-situ — a common one-mark NEET question.
